mismatch_files.summary: Comment claims to test 'value' account subdirective but no such subdirective is present in the test data; the six items listed (payee, value, default, note, check, eval) only five are actually exercised.

mismatch_files.summary: Comment claims to test define directive with expression, but my_var is never used — the test only exercises a plain bal command unrelated to the define.

issues.description_says: Test define directive with expression
issues.actually_tests: The define directive (my_var=42) is declared but never referenced or used anywhere in the transaction data or test command. The test only verifies a basic balance output for a simple $10 transaction; the defined variable plays no role in the result.

mismatch_files.summary: --now flag is placed after --end "next friday" (wrong order per fix applied to sibling dayofweek tests), making the reference epoch likely ineffective; additionally the test data has a gap between the two possible resolution dates so the specific claim of Mar 13 is unverifiable

issues.description_says: "next friday" resolved from --now 2026/03/03 (Tuesday) as the reference epoch
issues.actually_tests: --now appears AFTER --end "next friday" in the command, so the relative date is parsed before the epoch is set; the actual reference is the system date, not 2026/03/03

issues.description_says: "next friday" from --now 2026/03/03 resolves to 2026/03/13
issues.actually_tests: The test data has no transaction between 2026/03/06 and 2026/03/12, so the output is identical whether "next friday" resolves to Mar 6 or Mar 13 — the claimed resolution of Mar 13 cannot be verified from the test output

mismatch_files.summary: Argument ordering bug: --begin precedes --now, so --now may not control the "last wednesday" epoch resolution as the comment claims

issues.description_says: "last wednesday" from --now 2026/03/03 (Tuesday) resolves to 2026/02/25
issues.actually_tests: command has --begin "last wednesday" before --now 2026/03/03; per known Ledger behavior, --now must precede --begin for relative date parsing to use the overridden epoch — with this ordering, "last wednesday" may resolve from the system clock rather than from 2026/03/03

mismatch_files.summary: Header claims to test collapse_posts behavior with only_collapse_if_zero, but the test command omits --collapse so collapse_posts is never activated; the output is just normal register output

issues.description_says: Test collapse_posts with only_collapse_if_zero option
issues.actually_tests: The test command '--collapse-if-zero reg Expenses' does not include '--collapse', so the collapse_posts filter is never added to the processing chain; the option has no observable effect

issues.description_says: Covers: collapse_posts::report_subtotal() non-zero branch with only_collapse_if_zero

mismatch_files.summary: File is named 'equity-unround' but neither the description nor the test block exercises any unrounding behavior; the test simply verifies basic equity output with round numbers and no --unround flag.

issues.description_says: Test posts_as_equity filter (covers filters.h posts_as_equity class) - Exercises the equity command which generates opening balances
issues.actually_tests: The test does exercise the equity command and posts_as_equity filter, but the filename 'coverage-filter-equity-unround.test' strongly implies this file tests unrounded equity output (i.e., the --unround flag or unrounded amount behavior), which is neither described in the comments nor exercised in the test block

issues.description_says: The filename implies unrounded amounts are involved
issues.actually_tests: The test uses plain 'test equity' with no --unround flag, and all amounts in the expected output (5 AAPL, $-700.00, $700.00, -5 AAPL) are already round values - there is no rounding behavior being tested

mismatch_files.summary: --sort 'd' should produce date-ordered output but expected output is price-ordered ($145<$150<$155), contradicting the date-sort claim; description also overstates coverage of symbol, date, tag, and flag comparison branches

issues.description_says: --sort 'd' exercises date ordering in annotation_t::operator<
issues.actually_tests: Output is sorted by price ascending ($145 < $150 < $155), not by date: date ascending would give Lot A (Jan 1), Lot B (Jan 2), Lot C (Jan 3); price ascending gives Lot C, Lot A, Lot B which matches the expected output — the sort key 'd' (date) does not explain the observed ordering

issues.description_says: price commodity symbol comparison is exercised
issues.actually_tests: All three lot prices use the same commodity symbol ($), so the symbol comparison always returns equal and never acts as a tiebreaker; symbol-difference comparison is never reached

issues.description_says: date, tag, and flag ordering are exercised
issues.actually_tests: Since all three lot prices differ ($145, $150, $155), price-value comparison determines the order before date, tag, or flag comparisons are reached; those code paths are not exercised as distinguishing factors

mismatch_files.summary: Line numbers for check_command_has_match are misattributed (45-48 is the file header comment, not the function), and the warning path is not actually exercised since the named auto-xact always matches.

issues.actually_tests: Lines 45-48 in textual_xacts.cc are part of the file header doxygen comment listing parse_xact/parse_post/parse_posts — unrelated to check_command_has_match. That function is defined at lines 77-86.

issues.description_says: check_command_has_match warning path covered

mismatch_files.summary: The comment claims to exercise multi-field sort expressions, but the test data has unique payees so the secondary -amount sort key never affects ordering — the tie-breaking code path in compare_items is not actually covered

mismatch_files.summary: Description claims to test the check subdirective, but the single test block only verifies balance output for a conforming posting; no test covers what happens when the commodity constraint is violated, making the check directive incidental to the test outcome

issues.description_says: Test account directive with check subdirective
issues.actually_tests: Balance output with a valid posting; the check subdirective is present but its constraint is never exercised — removing it would produce identical test output

mismatch_files.summary: Primary mismatch: first test comment claims any() filters some accounts, but the expected output shows all accounts pass because any() is transaction-scoped; secondary issues are inconsistent line references in comments.

issues.description_says: Test any() returning true for some accounts, false for others
issues.actually_tests: any(amount > 0) returns true for ALL accounts — the expected output shows both Assets:Cash (-$30) and Expenses:Food ($30). In ledger, any() is transaction-scoped, so it is true for every posting because every transaction contains at least one positive posting (Expenses:Food). No account is filtered out.

issues.description_says: line 393 account.cc
issues.actually_tests: The header comments list coverage targets as lines 382, 400, 445, and 472 — line 393 is not mentioned there. The inline comment references a line number inconsistent with the header.

issues.actually_tests: None of the three test blocks obviously exercise account_t::lookup with a non-FUNCTION kind. The tests only use any()/all() predicates with balance; it is unclear which test is supposed to hit line 400.

issues.actually_tests: The test uses `account =~ /Food/` (the =~ regex operator), not the `account(/regex/)` function-call syntax

issues.actually_tests: Both test blocks only use account() which is a known/valid function; no test exercises an unknown function name that would trigger the nullptr fallthrough path

issues.description_says: Tests: account directives with sub-directives, account depth, fullname
issues.actually_tests: No test block exercises fullname functionality — no format string uses %(fullname), no --no-aliases flag, and no comparison between alias and fullname display. The accounts command shows canonical names by default, but that is not a targeted fullname test.

issues.description_says: account Expenses:Food with 'payee Grocery' sub-directive implies payee auto-matching is being tested
issues.actually_tests: The transaction uses the explicit 'food' alias for the posting, not payee auto-matching. The 'payee Grocery' sub-directive is declared but no test verifies that it causes Grocery transactions to auto-post to Expenses:Food.

mismatch_files.summary: Comment header claims to cover both 'apply fixed' and 'apply rate' directives but only 'apply fixed' is actually tested; the math and command are otherwise correct

issues.description_says: apply fixed / apply rate directive
issues.actually_tests: Only 'apply fixed' is used; 'apply rate' is a distinct Ledger directive that is not exercised anywhere in this test file

mismatch_files.summary: Comment incorrectly claims both directives go through switch case 'a'; 'check' starts with 'c' and would use a different case

issues.description_says: both 'assert' and 'check' directives are handled via general_directive switch case 'a'
issues.actually_tests: 'assert' begins with 'a' and would be switch case 'a', but 'check' begins with 'c' and would be switch case 'c' — two different switch cases are exercised, not one

mismatch_files.summary: Description accurately describes the auto_xact assert scenario but mischaracterizes the exception type as 'parse error' when Ledger actually throws a 'Transaction assertion failed' error

issues.description_says: a parse error is thrown
issues.actually_tests: a transaction assertion failure ('Transaction assertion failed: (amount < {0})') — which is an assertion_failed error, not a parse_error

mismatch_files.summary: Header claims to cover operator== and sorting comparisons, but operator== is never explicitly exercised and the second test block tests only output formatting, not sorting

issues.description_says: operator== for balance (equality comparison)
issues.actually_tests: Neither test block exercises an equality comparison; test 1 uses --sort total (ordering/operator<) and test 2 is a plain format output test with no sorting or equality

issues.description_says: Test focuses on balance comparison operations for sorting
issues.actually_tests: The second test block (bal --flat --no-total --format) is a straightforward formatting test that does not exercise sorting or comparison at all

mismatch_files.summary: Description says 'color name functions' but test also covers text formatting functions (bold, underline, blink) that are not color names

issues.description_says: color name functions (fn_black, fn_red, fn_green, etc.)
issues.actually_tests: The format string also exercises %(bold), %(underline), and %(blink) which are text formatting/attribute functions, not color name functions

mismatch_files.summary: Comment omits 'format' sub-directive, and the $ commodity's format/default directives are declared but not functionally exercised by any transaction in the test

issues.description_says: Commodity directive with nomarket, default, note, alias sub-directives
issues.actually_tests: Also tests the 'format' sub-directive (commodity $ uses 'format $1,000.00'), which is absent from the comment's list

issues.description_says: commodity $ with format $1,000.00 and default sub-directives is covered
issues.actually_tests: The $ commodity directives are declared but no transaction uses dollar amounts — the format and default sub-directives are parsed but their runtime behavior (formatting dollar values, using $ as default commodity) is never exercised or visible in the test output

mismatch_files.summary: Description implies the focus is balance assertion behavior with --effective, but the sole test block runs 'reg --effective' to verify register output in aux-date order; the balance assertion is incidentally present in the data but its interaction with --effective is not directly tested

issues.description_says: Test balance assertion with --effective using aux dates
issues.actually_tests: Register display using aux dates (--effective flag); the balance assertion '= $90.00' is present in the transaction data and is trivially satisfied, but no test block directly exercises or verifies balance assertion behavior with --effective (e.g., no test that the assertion is evaluated at the aux date, no error-case test showing assertion failure)

mismatch_files.summary: Expected output has 'Expense:Food:Groceries' (22 chars) where journal defines 'Expenses:Food:Groceries' (23 chars) — appears to be a typo rather than intentional column truncation since the truncation is inconsistent across entries; filename's 'edge' claim is not reflected in descriptions or test content

issues.description_says: account Expenses:Food:Groceries (from journal)
issues.actually_tests: expected output shows 'Expense:Food:Groceries' (missing trailing 's') — either a typo in expected output or the test would fail; same account name appears correctly in the journal as 'Expenses:Food:Groceries'

issues.description_says: account Expenses:Food:Groceries (from journal)
issues.actually_tests: expected output again shows 'Expense:Food:Groceries' (truncated) for two Grocery Store entries in test 2, while the Gas Station entry correctly shows 'Expenses:Auto:Gas' untruncated — the truncation is inconsistent and likely wrong

issues.description_says: filename says 'edge-predicate' implying edge cases are covered
issues.actually_tests: the comment only says 'complex predicate expressions' without specifying what edge cases are exercised; the tests use standard AND/OR compound predicates with =~ and >= operators, which are not particularly edge-case scenarios

mismatch_files.summary: Cost-path claim is accurate (lot annotation and cost column appear in output), but the 'note paths' claim is misleading: the transaction note is completely absent from the expected emacs output, so the test does not demonstrably verify any note-handling code path.

issues.description_says: exercises emacs.cc cost and note paths
issues.actually_tests: The transaction note '; Note: test note' on line 8 does not appear anywhere in the expected emacs output; the 'nil' slot in the 'Another Purchase' header (which could be the note field) is nil despite the note being present, so no note-specific output is verified

mismatch_files.summary: Test exercises market value balance display correctly, but filename implies rounding coverage that is absent, and the claimed 'changed_value_posts' code path is questionable for a plain bal --market command

issues.description_says: filename 'coverage-filter-display-rounding' implies rounding behavior is being tested
issues.actually_tests: plain market value conversion with no rounding edge cases, no --round option, and no scenarios where rounding would produce observable differences

issues.description_says: Covers display_filter_posts, changed_value_posts paths
issues.actually_tests: bal --market --exchange '$' on a stock with a price history; changed_value_posts is typically in the filter chain for register commands tracking value changes over time, not for a simple balance report

mismatch_files.summary: Test exercises unreduce path (7200s → 2.00h) but inline comment claims in_place_reduce is being exercised; header is more accurate by saying 'reduce/unreduce'.

issues.description_says: Reduce exercises in_place_reduce (lines 705-713)
issues.actually_tests: The test posts 7200s (already in base/smallest unit) and expects display as 2.00h, which is the in_place_unreduce direction (base → larger unit), not in_place_reduce (larger → base). in_place_reduce on 7200s would be a no-op since seconds are already the most reduced form.

mismatch_files.summary: Description implies period precedes comma ('period has already been seen'), but the test input $1,2345.67 has comma-then-period; the triggering sequence is the reverse of what the comment describes

issues.description_says: a comma is detected as a decimal separator (more than 3 digits follow it), but a period has already been seen — implying the period precedes the comma in the input
issues.actually_tests: $1,2345.67 has the comma before the period; when the comma is encountered no period has yet been seen; the period appears after the comma

mismatch_files.summary: Expected output shows Checking receiving -10 AAPL rather than -$1500.00, inconsistent with the 'Buy at fixated total cost' payee description and standard double-entry accounting for a cash purchase.

issues.description_says: Transaction payee 'Buy at fixated total cost' implies a cash-for-stock purchase where Assets:Checking receives -$1500.00 (the cash outflow)
issues.actually_tests: Expected output shows Assets:Checking as '-10 AAPL {=$150.00}' — the same commodity as Brokerage — which contradicts a cash purchase scenario. The total netting to 0 in AAPL units implies both accounts hold AAPL, not that cash was exchanged for stock.

mismatch_files.summary: Comment claims to test the D directive's default commodity effect, but expected output shows bare integers (-10, 10) without dollar formatting, making it unclear whether the D directive is actually being verified or its formatting effect silently absent.

issues.description_says: Test D directive (default commodity) — implies the D directive applies dollar commodity formatting to bare amounts
issues.actually_tests: The D directive is present but the expected output shows bare numbers (-10, 10) without the $-sign or decimal places, suggesting either the D directive is not applying its commodity format to the bare amount 10.00, or the test verifies that bare amounts remain unformatted despite the D directive

mismatch_files.summary: Comments focus exclusively on account masks but the test's distinguishing feature (per its filename) is date handling — specifically that 'on DATE' does not propagate to the template dump output, which shows '<today>' instead; description is incomplete/misleading about the test's actual focus

issues.description_says: Test draft template command with account masks (covers draft.cc lines 67-77)
issues.actually_tests: The test also explicitly exercises date handling: the command passes 'on 2024/03/01' but the expected output shows 'Date: <today>' — the date aspect is central to what's being tested (as the filename 'coverage-draft-template-date.test' makes clear), yet the comments never mention it

issues.description_says: Exercises the template dump function with account masks in postings
issues.actually_tests: The primary differentiator of this test from other template tests is the date behavior: despite specifying 'on 2024/03/01', the template output emits 'Date: <today>' — this behavior (that the 'on' date does not appear in the template dump output) is what the filename documents but what the comments entirely omit

mismatch_files.summary: Description says note applies to 'the initial post that matches the predicate' but output shows it applies to all three auto-xact-related postings (matched + both generated); the description undercounts the scope of propagation

issues.description_says: Tags on the auto-xact (before the postings) are applied to the initial post that matches the predicate
issues.actually_tests: The note appears on all three auto-xact-related postings (Expenses:Food, Liabilities:Tax, Assets:Tax), not just the initial matching post — the blank line in the output corresponds to Assets:Checking (no note), while all three auto-xact postings carry the note

mismatch_files.summary: Description claims test covers a 'blank sub-line break' in an automated transaction, but no such blank line appears in the auto-xact block; the note is also a header note (before postings), not a trailing note in the usual sense.

issues.description_says: Automated transaction with trailing note and blank sub-line break
issues.actually_tests: The automated transaction has a note sub-line (before the posting), but there is no blank sub-line break anywhere in the auto-xact block

mismatch_files.summary: Description overstates specificity: claims to test the CURRENT_TIME() fallback in map_prices but the test just lists all price entries with no time-sensitive expected output; the EUR transaction is also irrelevant noise

issues.description_says: Tests map_prices with CURRENT_TIME() fallback (line 79 triggered when no moment and no epoch)
issues.actually_tests: The test only verifies that 'prices EUR' lists all three price directives; it does not verify any behavior dependent on the current time — the expected output is fixed and would be identical regardless of whether CURRENT_TIME() or any other time value is used as the fallback

issues.description_says: The prices command calls map_prices with no explicit moment, triggering the CURRENT_TIME() fallback
issues.actually_tests: The prices command lists all P directives for EUR; the output is not time-bounded so there is no observable difference from the CURRENT_TIME() fallback vs any other fallback value

issues.description_says: Transaction payee 'Test' implies a meaningful test scenario
issues.actually_tests: The transaction (10 EUR purchase on 2024/01/15) is entirely unused by the 'prices EUR' command, which reads only P directives; the transaction data is extraneous and its payee describes nothing

mismatch_files.summary: Filename implies XML commodity flag serialization coverage, but the test uses `reg` (not `xml`), so the claimed coverage of commodity.cc lines 506-507 may be inaccurate if those lines are in an XML output path.

issues.description_says: Test commodity output with thousands separator (covers commodity.cc lines 506-507)
issues.actually_tests: The test uses the `reg` command to verify register output formatting. If commodity.cc lines 506-507 are in an XML serialization path (as the filename 'coverage-commodity-xml-flags' strongly implies), then the `reg` command would not exercise those lines at all.

issues.description_says: File name 'coverage-commodity-xml-flags' implies testing XML output of commodity flags
issues.actually_tests: Neither the test command (`reg`) nor any internal comment references XML output. The test only verifies that thousands-separator-formatted amounts appear correctly in register output. There is no `xml` command invocation anywhere in the file.
